topic: 3.1b Post-hoc vs Prospective — Our Standard vs the Author's

queue_context.reason: EXP-053 integrated — new Section 3.1b (Post-hoc vs Prospective) needs fresh adversarial review
queue_context.reviewer_note: Queue[0] (EXP-050 proposal) was already reviewed on 2026-04-09T22:52 by a prior curmudgeon run but the priority queue was never drained by the decider (priority-queue.json history still empty). Per 'one priority item per run' and 'do not duplicate work', I skipped re-reviewing EXP-050 and moved to queue[1]. Decider: please drain EXP-050 from the queue on your next run — the review file exists at reviews/EXP-050-proposal.json.

current_verdict_note: Section-level reviews do not have a verdict field; 'holds' here means 'the section is ready to ship as-is'. It is not — two major issues and one critical anchor bug require a revision pass before this section is safe.
our_argument_summary: Section 3.1b declares that the dome author's self-definition of 'prospective' (prediction committed to repo before data fetch executed) is too weak to be diagnostic, then substitutes a stricter two-clause standard: (a) temporal impossibility (data did not exist yet) OR (b) provable unawareness + independent timestamping. Adds a legitimate-novelty carve-out (3-condition AND gate), a trivial-transcription test (textbook values in indexed resources default to post-hoc), and a 'smoking gun' argument that hardcoded-monitoring WINs bake the expected value into the same commit as the data-fetch call.

holes_found.description:
The closing paragraph contains a broken self-reference anchor that will confuse every reader who clicks it. The text reads: 'See also CAT-005 / EXP-050 language on verdict criteria for the V51.1 refraction cascade'. But #posthoc-standard is the id of THIS VERY SECTION (3.1b's own

). Clicking the link jumps the reader back to the top of the same section they are reading. There is no CAT-005 / EXP-050 content at that anchor.
holes_found.root_cause: The author of EXP-053 preemptively wrote a cross-reference to Section 1.7 (the planned CAT-005 / V51.1 refraction cascade section), but Section 1.7 does not yet exist (it is still under curmudgeon review at the proposal-package stage — EXP-050 is currently in queue[0] with major revisions required). Rather than omitting the reference until the target section exists, the author pointed the href at #posthoc-standard as a placeholder — but that is the current section's own id.
holes_found.recommendation: Remove the entire sentence 'See also ... same timestamping bar.' from the closing paragraph. Do not point at any anchor. When Section 1.7 is eventually written (with a different id, e.g. #v51-cascade), the cross-reference can be added back as part of integrating that section. Shipping a section that contains a link to itself labeled as pointing elsewhere is a credibility hit — any skim-reader who follows the link will conclude we are sloppy.

holes_found.description: The dome site explicitly claims cryptographic pre-registration via OpenTimestamps anchored to the Bitcoin blockchain. From raw-text/03-predictions.txt lines 7-8 and 79-80: 'Cryptographically timestamped — falsifiable — pre-registered' and 'OpenTimestamps: Eclipse predictions anchored to Bitcoin blockchain.' Our Section 3.1b clause (b) says 'a commit hash alone is insufficient because commits can be rewritten; an external notarization, a published paper, or a third-party hash anchor is required.' OpenTimestamps anchored to Bitcoin IS exactly a third-party hash anchor. A dome defender who reads our section will immediately say: 'You demand third-party hash anchoring as the bar and we provide it — your own standard admits our predictions are prospective.' The section currently contains no response to this argument.
holes_found.why_it_matters: This is the single most effective rebuttal a dome defender can construct against Section 3.1b, and the author of the section has left us rhetorically unarmed against it. The author clearly knew about OpenTimestamps (it is prominent on the dome home page and in the raw-text/ we audit) and chose not to address it. Every reader who knows even a little about blockchain anchoring will wonder why we ignored it.
holes_found.recommendation: Add a new paragraph — roughly 120-180 words — that does the following in order: (1) acknowledge that the dome uses OpenTimestamps and that this satisfies the 'third-party hash anchor' half of our clause (b); (2) explain that clause (b) has TWO parts, and the anchor only satisfies the second — the first requires 'provable unawareness of the confirming value at the time the prediction was written'; (3) give the Schumann example: the 7.83 Hz fundamental has been in textbooks since Schumann 1952, so a Bitcoin-anchored 2026 commit setting pred=7.83 timestamp-proves WHEN the line was written but not THAT the author was unaware of the value, and the latter is the operative clause; (4) note that OpenTimestamping genuinely rescues one specific class of WINs — the August 2026 eclipse station-amplitude predictions (WIN-044, E001-E009 station list), because the confirming eclipse data does not yet exist and clause (a) 'temporal impossibility' therefore genuinely applies once the eclipse is observed. Concede this openly — it is an honest concession that STRENGTHENS the review rather than weakens it by showing we distinguish real prospective claims from retrodictions. (5) End by saying that every OTHER WIN that invokes OpenTimestamps still fails because the confirming data was already public. This turns a hole in the section into a structural argument that we have carefully-calibrated criteria, not an ideological rejection of anything the author does.

holes_found.description: The 'smoking gun' paragraph (hardcoded expected values in the same commit as the live fetch) is presented as refuting the author's self-definition of prospective. But it does not. The author defines prospective as 'prediction was timestamped before confirming data was pulled.' The smoking gun shows that the PREDICTION (hardcoded number) was written in the same commit as the FETCH CODE — but both still predate any actual PULL of confirming data at runtime. Under the author's definition, the timing chain is still intact: commit lands at t0, cron runs fetch at t1 > t0, data arrives at t1, comparison happens at t1. The author will say: 'Yes, the prediction was baked into the script, that is exactly what a prospective prediction looks like when it is committed to code before the measurement runs. Your own description confirms our definition is satisfied.'
holes_found.why_it_matters: As currently written, the smoking gun section overreaches. It claims to show 'there is no asymmetry in time between prediction and confirmation — they are literally adjacent lines.' But adjacent-in-source is not adjacent-in-time; the fetch runs at runtime, not at commit time. The real argument we are making (correctly) is not about timing — it is about KNOWLEDGE: the author wrote pred=7.83 because they already knew 7.83 was the answer. That is an argument about awareness, not about commit timing. Presenting it as a timing argument lets the defender win on timing and ignore the awareness point.
holes_found.recommendation: Rewrite the smoking gun paragraph to pivot on knowledge, not timing. Suggested direction: 'The author's timing-based definition is unfalsifiable for hardcoded-monitoring WINs not because the timestamps are wrong (they may well be correct) but because the confirming VALUE is already present in the predicting commit. A cryptographic timestamp can prove that a script containing pred=7.83 existed at time t0. It cannot prove that the author did not already know 7.83 at time t0. For any WIN whose target value is a widely known textbook number, the author's own definition is satisfied vacuously — the prediction exists in the repo before the data fetch runs — while being diagnostically empty: the monitoring commit is effectively a transcription of an already-known answer. This is why our stricter standard demands PROVABLE UNAWARENESS in clause (b), not just independent timestamping.' This framing is both more charitable to the author and much harder to rebut, because it targets the actual argumentative weakness (authored-while-knowing) rather than an irrelevant timing quibble.

holes_found.description: The carve-out requires all three conditions (unique derivation + plausible unawareness + published-together). This is a three-condition AND gate, and the prose says 'We have not found a WIN that clears this bar.' A dome defender will point out that this is unfalsifiable in the other direction: no realistic WIN can meet a three-condition AND gate simultaneously, so the carve-out is ornamental — it exists to make us look charitable but never actually fires.
holes_found.concern: If the carve-out is effectively dead letter, it invites the 'stacked deck' criticism. The review's credibility rests on applying symmetric, calibrated standards. A standard that nothing can meet is not a standard; it's a rejection clause dressed up as openness.
holes_found.recommendation: EITHER relax condition (3) — published-together is too tight for an informal website that continually updates — OR drop the carve-out entirely and replace with a simpler 'if a dome WIN satisfies (a) or (b) we will mark it Prospective; no WIN in the current review has done so, but we are open to it.' The simpler version is honest and does not pretend to a charitable carve-out that never fires. Editorial preference is the latter, but either is fine.

holes_found.description: Clause (b) requires pre-registration 'in a venue that timestamps independently of the predictor.' A dome defender will note that the author is an outsider by choice and design, and that peer-reviewed venues are gatekept and unlikely to accept a flat-Earth cosmology as a preprint. If the only valid timestamping venues are 'journal, preprint server, or third-party hash anchor', and the dome has ONLY the third option (Bitcoin/OpenTimestamps), then the author is effectively being told 'your one valid mechanism is insufficient unless you pair it with a venue that will never accept you.' This is an easy 'systemic bias' attack.
holes_found.recommendation: Either make the venue list fully inclusive of third-party hash anchors as stand-alone (which is almost what the current prose does but it buries the point) OR explicitly state in the text: 'A third-party hash anchor is by itself sufficient to satisfy the timestamping clause — no journal publication is required. The reason OpenTimestamped dome predictions still fail clause (b) is that the OTHER half of clause (b) — provable unawareness of the confirming value — is not satisfied for textbook constants.' Pair this with the OpenTimestamps preemption (major hole 2). Together they fully neutralize the outsider critique.

holes_found.description: The 'student could prospectively predict the boiling point of water tomorrow morning and claim a WIN tomorrow afternoon' analogy is rhetorically effective but veers into mocking. Part 4 (Methodology) commits us to Principle 6 (charitable engagement) and our earlier holistic TONE review (HOLISTIC-TONE) flagged exactly this kind of dismissive-chuckle framing as a risk.
holes_found.recommendation: Replace the analogy with a neutral physics example: 'Under the author's rule, a student who writes a Python script containing pred=100 before running a water-boiling experiment at 1 atm has made a prospective prediction; the timestamp is valid; the result confirms. No serious observer would call this a scientific prediction, because the value is public knowledge and the student knew it. The diagnostic we want is not timing but whether the predicted value was already known.' This carries the same force with no implied condescension.

holes_found.description: The author-quote is cited as '— john09289.github.io/predictions, home page, 2026-04-09.' Today is 2026-04-10 — fine now — but the dome site is a moving target. If the author re-words the prospective definition (which they have done across V50→V51.1 for other claims), the quote becomes stale and the author can accuse us of arguing against a definition they no longer use.
holes_found.recommendation: Add a one-line footnote or parenthetical: '(We archived the quoted language at raw-text/03-predictions.txt, commit hash <SHA>, which is referenced by Part 2b for the code audit as well. If the dome home page rewords this definition, our rebuttal still holds against the archived version; we will update the citation if a substantive change occurs.)' This protects against the 'moved goalposts' attack by preemptively anchoring the quoted text.

holes_found.description: The smoking-gun paragraph uses {{CA_HARDCODED}} as a placeholder count. Verified in generate-html.js line 76: '{{CA_HARDCODED}}': context.codeAnalysis?.monitoring?.hardcoded — exists. No defect.

Clause (b) says 'a commit hash alone is insufficient because commits can be rewritten.' Git commits cannot be rewritten once pushed to a shared remote without a force-push that leaves a dangling reflog entry, and GitHub records force-pushes in the repo audit log. This is a minor technical imprecision that a git-literate defender will seize on.
holes_found.recommendation: Soften to 'a commit hash alone is insufficient because the author controls both the repo and the commit history, and force-push with history rewrite is available to a motivated actor.' Or simpler: 'a commit hash alone is insufficient because it is authored by the same party whose unawareness it would need to prove — the timestamp and the predictor are not independent.' The independence framing is cleaner than the 'can be rewritten' framing.

kernel_of_truth.description: Section 3.1b is doing important methodological work that the review badly needs. The author's definition of 'prospective' is genuinely permissive, the textbook-constant examples are genuinely damning, and stating our stricter standard explicitly (rather than leaving it implicit in per-WIN reviews) is exactly the right editorial move. The (a)+(b) framing with the temporal-impossibility clause as the clean escape route is sound. The August 2026 eclipse WINs (if they survive) will genuinely clear that bar. The section as a whole represents the kind of methodological self-consciousness that our Part 4 (Methodology) commits us to.
kernel_of_truth.why_revisions_are_still_required: The section has real analytical teeth but ships with a broken anchor, a missing preemption of the strongest dome defense (OpenTimestamps), and a smoking-gun argument that targets the wrong axis (timing, not knowledge). These are fixable in 200-400 words of edits. None of them threaten the section's main argument; all of them threaten its credibility on first contact with a motivated skeptic.

cross_win_consistency.notes: Section 3.1b is consistent with the existing Part 2b code audit (hardcoded-vs-live tag breakdown) and with Part 4 methodology (charitable engagement, falsifiability). The future Section 1.7 (if EXP-050 is eventually written) will need to reuse the timestamping-bar language from 3.1b; the author anticipated this correctly but jumped the gun with the cross-reference.

claims_checked: Verified {{CA_HARDCODED}} placeholder registration in build-scripts/generate-html.js (line 76) — present.
Verified #posthoc-standard anchor appears exactly once in data/sections.json (as the <h3> id), confirming the self-reference bug.
Verified OpenTimestamps / Bitcoin claim in raw-text/03-predictions.txt lines 7-8, 79-80.
quantitative_verification.computations: N/A — this section contains methodological prose, not numerical claims. All factual checks were against the codebase and raw-text archives.

advocate_mode.best_defense: A fair-minded dome defender, having read this section, would write: 'You acknowledge that our predictions are cryptographically timestamped via OpenTimestamps on the Bitcoin blockchain — except you don't acknowledge it, because you wrote an entire methodology section about pre-registration standards without mentioning the mechanism we actually use. You demand a third-party hash anchor and we provide exactly that. You say commit hashes can be rewritten; OpenTimestamps anchoring to Bitcoin makes that impossible. Either your stricter standard is satisfied by our methodology (in which case our claims are prospective by your own rule and you owe us a correction) or you have a hidden clause 'not if we don't trust you' that you have not stated. Show the work: which specific clause of your clause (b) do we fail, given that we have the notarization?'
advocate_mode.preemptive_recommendation: The hole is the OpenTimestamps omission (major hole 2 above). Add the preemption paragraph as described. After that, the advocate's defense is fully answered by the 'provable unawareness' half of clause (b), not by timestamping. The defense survival rating drops from 4 to 1.

citations_verified: raw-text/03-predictions.txt lines 7-8, 79-80 (OpenTimestamps / Bitcoin anchoring — confirmed present)
Section 3.1a analytical tags table (referenced correctly as the previous section)
Part 2b code audit (forward reference via #part2b — anchor exists)
citations_failed: #posthoc-standard cross-reference claiming to point at 'CAT-005 / EXP-050 language' — points at this section's own header. See critical hole 1.
citations_unchecked: john09289.github.io/predictions home page quote — relied on raw-text archive, did not re-fetch the live site. See minor hole 7 for mitigation.

action_gating: One critical (self-anchor removal) and two major (OpenTimestamps preemption, smoking-gun reframing) edits are required before this section is safe to leave in the published review. Three moderate edits (carve-out tightening, venue-requirement clarification, analogy tone) are strongly recommended. Two minor edits (quote archival anchor, commit-hash phrasing) are polish. Total revision budget: approximately 300-500 words of additions and replacements. I recommend the decider create a patches file for these edits and the curmudgeon re-check on the next cycle.
gating_conditions_before_clean_bill: 1. Remove the #posthoc-standard self-reference from the closing paragraph (critical).
3. Rewrite smoking-gun paragraph to target knowledge, not timing (major).
4. Consider relaxing or dropping the three-condition legitimate-novelty carve-out (moderate).
5. Clarify that a third-party hash anchor alone satisfies the timestamping clause, and pair with provable-unawareness explanation (moderate).
6. Swap the student-boiling-point analogy for a neutral physics framing (moderate).
7. Add a short footnote archiving the dome quote's source with a file path and (if possible) a commit SHA (minor).
deeper_analysis:
This section is fundamentally sound and ships with exactly one critical bug (a self-referencing anchor that any reader can find in ten seconds) and one major missing preemption (OpenTimestamps) that the single most informed dome defender will spot instantly. Both are the kind of error that happens when a section is written in isolation — the author knew the cross-reference target (EXP-050) did not exist yet and used a placeholder anchor, probably intending to fix it later; and the author knew OpenTimestamps existed on the dome site but thought it was a side-issue not worth addressing in a general methodology section. Both judgment calls are wrong in ways that become obvious when the section is read by an adversary.

On the other side of the argument (per user preference): the dome author's own self-definition of prospective IS genuinely too permissive. A definition that admits 'I committed pred=7.83 to a script before the fetch ran' as prospective evacuates the word of meaning. Our stricter standard, once cleaned up, is the correct methodological stance. The reason to be hard on Section 3.1b is not that the underlying argument is wrong but that it is RIGHT and we are giving it weak rhetorical packaging. A section about methodological rigor cannot ship with a broken anchor. A section about pre-registration standards cannot ignore the dome's own pre-registration mechanism. Both are unforced errors.

The OpenTimestamps concession is actually a strategic gift: acknowledging that WIN-044 and the August 2026 eclipse station-amplitude predictions DO clear clause (a) once the eclipse happens is both honest and sharp, because it shows our standard has edges rather than being a blanket rejection. A dome defender who successfully backs us into saying 'yes, under our rule that eclipse WIN will be prospective after August 12, 2026' has not won anything — they have agreed to our rule and committed to a falsification path. The review is strictly stronger for having this concession in writing. The section's current silence on the point leaves the concession implicit and loses its strategic value.
